speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Gedis. You were telling us that there was no way to run away in Omsk while you were there. Why don't you just describe to us what the winters were like while you were in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said before, you know, you got-- about nine months you got winter, you know? In Russia you got some rain, you know, bad weather. Very seldom, you know, the sun is warm over there, because it snowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's real cold, you know? That's what they call Siberia. This is the worst place for the Russians. You name the word Siberia, they know what it is, you know. [INAUDIBLE] So, Mr. Gedis, how would you say your health was at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My health? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Except a frozen toe, I was in good health. Yeah. I froze my toe, because the shoes were not warm enough, you know, to wear. I had to work outside. And still, years later, I have the itching, you know, and all that. But, otherwise, I was young and I was strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have medical care there if you needed it, if anyone needed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people dying around you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some people died. Yes. Yeah, some people died. We didn't even ask for that, how, why, the reason, the what, you know? What had happened, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave this work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we left it when the war was over. That was in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. So I had to have a permit, you know, which I went over. We had a permit. They called me to KGB, you know, and they say, why you want to leave Russia? Don't you like it here? I say, I love it. I just want to go, because I left a family over there, a father, a mother, and three sisters. And I want to go and see, maybe they're alive. So, finally, they give me that permit. It was an international law, by the way. They didn't have to do that. Was a law. If people are in other countries, you know, they got a right to go back to their home, you know. Got to have a permit to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, finally, they give me that permit, you know. And with a train, you know, it went to the Polish border. They put me on the train. How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took about a week after that. And, as I said, you know, I used to write this diary about the people, you know, that I knew, and the sketches that I made, and everything. And I took it with me. I thought, well, I'll have something to show for people, you know, and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't work out, you know, because, as I said, the KGB stepped in inside. I saw with the other side on the train, you know? And they stepped in. They start asking people open up their luggage, you know, what they have, the few things they have. They're looking for some stuff that's against the Communist-- Communist Party, you know, against their laws, against their ideals.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that if they found this diary with me, I'll be sent back to Siberia, because this is anti-Russian, to write the truth about them. Also, the people who were involved there, the names that I had, it's very bad for them too. So, as I said before, they were at the other side of the train. They didn't see me. So I opened up the window a little bit and threw it out, the whole thing. That way, I came back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first thing I stopped was the little town where I lived, you know, Zdolbunow. What did you find when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found something like you going in a desert, you know, and you find here and there, you know, a-- something that grows wild, you know. You don't know what it is and everything strange. People, you know, you don't know. People are walking by, you know. You look at them. You don't even know them. The neighborhood where I lived, you know, I went through. I say, I want to go to my house where I lived and see. I go to that place. You know, I didn't want to knock at the door. I didn't know what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I look in the window. Maybe I'll find some face that I know, somebody. And there was nothing. The guy looked out to me, he says, what do you want? You know, a Russian. I say, I'm sorry. I made a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Russian well. So I made a mistake. Nobody was there, none of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't know nothing. Except, one of the guys was with them over there and he says, how in the world did you survive? How did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody's gone. How did you survive? So I understood with his words, you know, what happened to people here. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was my coming. Then I saw that I don't have nothing to do in this place. I kept on going, you know? Walking, going. I found some people with horses, you know? I asked them, I begged him. Take me to Rivne, you know. Over there, you know? So they took me. They didn't know who I am, you know. They took me over there. And from there, you know, traveling here and there. And we got in touch with some people that worked for Jewish organizations, you know, finally, in Rivne. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what month? End of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- can't remember the month, but it was summertime, you know. So we got in touch with them, and they told us that in Przemysl, you know, a little town near Russia, near the German-- not far from the German border. The organizations, they're organizing people, you know, to go to Israel, kibbutzim. I tired everything to do, you know. I didn't have money, I didn't have nothing, you know. So it took me weeks and weeks. I didn't have nothing to eat. But these Jewish organizations, you know, trying to help a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which organizations were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember what they are. But they were working-- people were, you know, working free, you know. They helped out, you know, much as they could. And they got help, probably, from somebody, from superiors, from somebody else, probably. And they gave us a little food. They also gave us a possibility to reach Przemysl, you know, which was the center of the young people, you know, that wanted to go to Israel. Well, there I got in touch with the people. We-- places. There was a big anti-Semitism those days, you know. They used to rob places, the jumping, you know, and shoot, you know, with guns.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are the reaction after the Communists, you know, invaded Poland, you know. There was a reaction between the Polacks, the Polish people, you know. So we were careful. We had some ammunition and we had some arms to protect ourselves, which they gave us, the Jewish people, you know? Did you ever have to use it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't, but we were ready for them, yes. So that's one of the things. And the other things, I found out what it is, you know? When they start organizing people, you know, in groups to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel was under the English mandate, if you remember at that time? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there's no communication. No this, no that, you know. We had to go. We had to go through the Alps. They took us to a certain point with a bus, and that's the rest, you had to go to the Alps. The other side, somebody will wait for you. It was very good organized, from Italy, Jewish organizations from Italy. And the other side will wait for you, but you got to reach the other side, you know? So I had to buy some cigarettes, you know. They gave us some packages of cigarettes, you know, and that could buy everything, freedom and everything. So we gave the guards, when we passed the Austrian border, we gave them some of these things. And they let us go. Go away. As long as we got our cigarettes, go. So we reached northern Italy. It's beautiful over there. We're waiting for the train. They made me the head of the group, you know. We had about 39-- 39 people, I think we had. So I came over to a guy-- I didn't know Italian. Later, I learned a little Italian. I came over to the guy and I talked to him. You speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we speak German. So I started in German, you know. Told him we want to go to Rome. And we gave him a few packs of cigarettes and everything. There was a policeman standing-- a carabiniere they called him, carabiniere. And he was with them. You know, they divided the stuff between them, and says, OK. A train is coming in an hour, so wait. They will take you over there. So an hour later, they came to come see, you know, and a bunch of nuns from the Vatican tried to jump in into that place where they promised us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the carabiniere came over to him and he says, hold a second. Where's the [ITALIAN]. Everything is taken here. And he took them out, they chased them all out, you know. They put us in for the few cigarettes that we gave them, you know, the few boxes, and we went inside, you know. And that way, we reached Rome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we reached Rome, you know. Right away, they had a place for us in a place called Gallicano nel Lazio. This is close to Rome, not far, where the Pope has got this private place, you know, to live. Not far. In the mountains. Very nice place. And we had this place over there. We start to organize, holidays, you know, and this and that. They helped us a lot. I had to learn a little Italian, because we had to register the people, who they are here. There was a law in Italy came out, that people come out from abroad, they got to be registered with the city hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small place, the city hall in this little town. So I brought them a little wine, a little this and that, you know, and that talked for itself. That was the best language to talk to them, you know? And they gave us permission, you know. I told them we got 30 more people, 40 more people, you know, and this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time, people used to come in. So one day, they sent up carabinieres, you know, to check how many people. You know, a lot of people left already. We didn't have too many. They came, you know, to check if these people are there. You know, they still live there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we did, we had to change clothes, you know. People changed clothes. They came out every time from the room. What's your name? So-and-so. What's your name? So-and-so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then you came, so different clothing, different hat, and we changed the names. And this way, we kept them straight so the government wouldn't bother us, you know, until we leave Italy. And that was the story with Italy. We were there-- can't remember exactly, but I think about seven, eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in this period of time, did you become aware of what happened in Europe with the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. We knew about it. It was a terrible thing, you know. I was depressed. For many months, I was depressed, you know, when I couldn't find my father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able--

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out later, in Cyprus. When we come to that, I'll tell you. I found out later a little about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when it came time to leave with your group from Italy, how did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left from Bari, you know, which is south Italy. And that was organized, you know. The Jewish organizations gave us, you know, the possibility to reach over there. We came to a little boat. It was a fisherman's boat, a small fisherman's boat. Was wintertime. And we hardly could get in that boat, you know. It was wintertime, and the boat was jumping up and down when they start-- start going. We got--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got sick over there, seasick. And still, today, you know, when I go on the airplane, I'm getting seasick, too. [LAUGHS] And we reached the-- yeah, it took us, what? About a day or two days, I think. We reached close by to Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, we see three big ships, you know. Warships, English. Surrounded us. Came out with the guns, and, you know, the automatic, all kind of things. Stop, stop, stop. So our guy threw them a can of candies. You know, everybody was sick. Everybody wanted to come, already, to a place and start living their life. Threw them a bunch of-- of food, you know, and they thought it's a bomb or something. They shot him right away, you know. He came, walked by, they shot-- a young fellow, you know. They shot and killed him right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they jumped on the ship, took us, everybody, you know. You're going back. Where are we going? We see Haifa already, right here. The lights already. Ready. That's the freedom. That's where we're going.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, you're going to come with us. You're going. Eventually, they took us to Cyprus. So we came to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were on this boat, were you with anyone that you knew? Anybody in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They all were from all kind of the world, you know, all kind of people, you know. Didn't know. But I had the group of my group that I had, you know, with them. 39 people, you know. We knew each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they all refugees from--

speaker: interviewee
text: All refugees. All of them. That's why we organized in Przemysl in-- in-- in Poland. Yeah. And that's when we came to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you both women and men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women and men. She was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just forgot to mention my wife, you know. We got married. She was on another kibbutz with another group. But this group fell apart, because a lot of people went back to Europe, ran back. People went back to their home. Lot of people got to stay in Italy, and the rest of them got to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw that they broke up. You know, I say, come to me. You know, I liked her. She was nice, you know, and everything. We talked a few times, not with a language, with a hand, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. She didn't know the language, you know. She didn't know Yiddish. Speaks Hungarian and a little German, spoke the-- and she came over to our kibbutz. And she was with us. So we traveled together until Cyprus. And in Cyprus they put them in a camp. That's why I married her. A Romanian rabbi gave us the marriage, you know, everything fine. And that's it. We spend the first night in a tent, about 10, 12 people. Was a big rain, you know. I remember, the tent was on a little hill. You know, water used to come in from one side, and running out the other side. So that was the beginning of that, you know. Then we find out next day what a camp it is. Huge camps. They got a winter camp and a summer camp. So we-- the food, and this, everything was limited.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, finally, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a few Jewish people that were taking care on the-- talking to people, you know, explaining them and all that. They asked me and I told them who I am and everything. How would you like to be with us as a teacher for children? I say, I'll be glad to do it. So I had a little extra food, you know, a little extra this, and that, you know. So I start teaching. I still have the-- I showed you that writing, you know, that I was doing a good job with the children, you know, when I was teaching them, teaching them, you know, Hebrew. And that's where we were until we were out of Cyprus. How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: About over a year. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Jewish community form there? Did you have your own little government?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they used to come to visit. Golda Meir was once over there. They came to visit us. They couldn't do much, but the English were very, very--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I was surprised after the war. The English worked together with the Americans, you know, and other free countries. And after the war, they were so rude, you know? It was terrible. It was terrible. Very rude. Then later, they checked everything and every little thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a time we tried to escape. There wasn't. They caught us, you know. So nowhere near. They never helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the time came, how did you finally get to leave the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we came to the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, how did you get to leave the camp, finally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally? Officially, because Israel-- we heard that night-- was a rainy night, terrible night. You know, we were outside, you know, talking to each other. I mean, near the tents. And, all of a sudden, we heard the news. Somebody came over and broke the news to us that Israel became a state, you know? That's how. You can't imagine what a night it was, you know. Everybody was dancing in the street. Everybody was wet. Water was running all over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who care? After going through all those things. You know, losing your family, sisters, and parents, you know? You come over, you say, I've got my own country. I can start a new living over there, a new family. You know what it is? People didn't sleep all night. We were dancing, I remember, all night. At that point, had you found out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found a girl-- the children-- one of the families from out town, you know. But she was in camp. A while, she was in camp. And then, she-- somehow, one of the Christians got in touch with her and took her away. I don't know how in our-- it wasn't a camp. It was in our town, you know, where they kept them together in one place. Then they dig the-- you know, diggings. They kill them over there, all together. But a woman came over, a Polish, and she asked, she needs somebody to clean the house, you know. Maybe this-- this girl if she's good. She knew the girl. And this girl survived, you know, somehow. It was not a camp. In a camp, she wouldn't do that. It was in Zdolbunow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the heck with that, the guy said. OK, take her. But don't let her go nowhere. You're responsible for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took her, and she kept on until the end of the war. She survived. And she told me that my father was the first one that was killed. In our town, a German was killed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- Actually, the Ukrainians killed him, but they said the Jews killed him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took the rabbi from town, they took two more Jewish people, and they took my father, you know, first one. They took him to the middle of the market, you know, in they shot him over there before the people. That's the payment for the German that you killed, you know. And that's what she told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: About my mother and my sister, she didn't know. She know one thing, you know, my mother lost her mind, at that time. She didn't know nothing. She lost the children, too. They took away the children apart, and she was apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she didn't know about mother. That's all she told me. That's all she knew. And you never heard anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard about it. I don't even know the place where they're buried, where they are. Don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you finally arrive in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we arrived in-- that's '48, end of '48, beginning of '49. Something like that, yeah. We arrived at Israel, near Haifa. And we started over there, you know, to get organized a little bit. They put us in another camp, but that was another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a camp, a temporary living, you know. They gave us food and everything. You know, all the facilities they had. And until we getting established, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, finally, with the time, you know, I had cousins over there, here and there, so we moved out and we rent a place, you know. They helped us out. And I start working for a company, one of the biggest company in Israel, [INAUDIBLE], which is doing a lot of buildings, constructions, and all kind of stuff, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the man that was supplying them parts for the machines and all that stuff, because I knew English and knew a little bit about these things. So they made me over there. They gave me a good job, and I start working over there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Israel, did you have to serve in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not have to serve, but they took me as a miluim. That means not the regular army--

speaker: interviewer
text: Reservist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reserve army. They took me. And I didn't have to go-- every year, I had to go for a week or two. When the war broke out, you know, over there, they took me to the army, with the, you know, Holy War. I showed you that thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I think, '48, '49, I think. 1949. And I was taken to the army They took me to Jerusalem. I was there with the army. And then they took me to the Golan Heights. Remember the night we went through over there. They were shooting at us, you know, from the mountains, and making fun. Because they were sitting up there high, you know, and we were down there. And I spent quite a lot of time, you know, down there. I remember, was Mountain Gilboa-- there's a Mountain Gilboa over there. And we came there, you know. We had to stay over there a few days to wait until we got new orders, where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I look around at the Gilboa and say, there's nothing in there. Everything is naked, you know? I mean, nothing grows here. And then, I remember, you know, the Bible said, you know, about Saul-- King Saul, you know, when his helper, you know, adjutant, you know, died, and he was very, very much nervous about it. He was very much upset that he died. So he said about the Gilboa I wish God will never let nothing grow on this mountain here anymore. And I was thinking about it. It's interesting, you know, how it's connected to the Bible. That's what I was told. Yeah, that was the army, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the story about the army, you know. After the war, we settled down. I start working as I worked, again, for this company. And it wasn't so bad. We made a living over there. Was not bad, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Got children. One is Rachel, and one is Steve.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born-- what-- what is it? '47? About '49, '50. Something like that, yeah. She was born in '50. My son was born later, a couple of years, yeah. And that was it. I left my wife with a child, you know. She was pregnant. And I had to go to the army. And nobody knew where we're going, because it was kept a secret, you know. Nobody was supposed to know what  we're doing. That was Israel. But the situation was bad. Two children, little children, and there wasn't enough to eat in those days, you know. We didn't have enough food for the children, and this and that. My wife was working and I was working. And I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Israel. I wanted to stay there. Believe me, I wanted to stay there, you know? But she didn't. She had problems with the language. Didn't know the language. And two children, and all that. And she had a cousin here. She wanted to come to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tried to talk her out of it, and this, and that. I'd say, it's our country. We're here. We went through plenty. You lost your parents. You lost your family, I'd say. And I lost my family. Let's stay here. No, she's got a cousin here, which she was very close to her. They grew up together as little children, you know, her cousin. Two cousins, as a matter of fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she sent her papers. I'll send you papers. Come over here. Come over here. We'll help you. And that's the way we came here, you know? It was a bad situation, Israel, in that time, with children especially. So when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good question. I got to look at my papers. Yeah-- Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it around 1962?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, how did you know? 1962, that's right. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to Boston. I had an uncle over there. We stayed over a little while, and we said, well, we have to go further than that, you know? Hollywood was my dream, you know. Go to that crazy Hollywood, you know? So let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her cousin lived here, you know, in Los Angeles. So finally-- you know, my uncle was good to me. He was nice and everything. But we said, we have to go and settle down, and say, finally, start a new life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came here to Los Angeles, you know. And we stayed with her cousin for quite awhile, until we came here in Los Angeles. She lived at, actually, outside Los Angeles, near the-- near the harbor, you know, she lives, her cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she start working a little bit. I start working for the people and a little bit, you know. We made a little money and start living. And then we opened up a little place, you know, a cafeteria. And with that, we stayed for a while, yeah. That's just about the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for you to adjust because of the language and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not for me. Because I knew the language. I knew the language not bad. I knew the language before that. I studied before. I like languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied Latin, too, and that's why I made easier for me. I speak Spanish, too, you know? So that's easier for me, you know? Because a lot of words come from Latin. You just spell them different, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I didn't have problems, you know. No. Citizenship and all that stuff, no. So she got it with me. Together, you know, we went through the whole thing, you know. And then we changed our name. That's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the way it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we bought a little business, a cafeteria. We stayed in it, you know, quite a while. And it was hard. Was time to leave the job, you know? Relax. We relaxed a little bit, and what am I going to do? I enrolled a health club here, you know. Going to the health club, I met, over there, some actors, big actors, you know, and everything. And what do we do? What am I doing, I say. What am I going to do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love Hollywood. I would like to-- I'll tell you. I'll give you a address. Go to a guy over there. He'll give you-- go over there and he'll give you a job, you know, as an extra.

speaker: interviewee
text: So better than doing nothing, you know? So I start with that, you know? Let's stop right there, and we'll change the film. And we'll talk about it again.
